paragraphs: On this bench, gas pressure comes from particles colliding with the walls. The ideal gas law is the compact rule that links that pressure to particle number, temperature, and volume.

Temperature sets the average particle-speed scale, particle number sets how many particles can hit the walls, and volume sets how often they get back to the walls. Faster particles, more particles, or a smaller box all make pressure rise.

This is a simplified 2D model rather than a full gas simulator, but it keeps the main ideal-gas patterns honest. It also shows that the same pressure can come from different microscopic situations, so pressure alone does not tell you temperature or particle number by itself.

keyIdeas: At fixed particle number and temperature, reducing volume raises pressure because the particles hit the walls more often in the smaller box.
At fixed particle number and volume, raising temperature raises pressure because particles move faster and transfer more momentum in each collision.
At fixed temperature and volume, adding particles raises pressure even though the average speed of each particle stays about the same.
The same pressure can come from different combinations of particle speed and crowding, so pressure alone does not determine temperature or particle number.

commonMisconception.myth: Pressure only depends on how crowded the gas is, so heating matters only if the number of particles changes.
correction: Crowding matters, but temperature matters too because hotter particles move faster and hit the walls harder.
That is why a hotter gas at the same particle number and volume can have a higher pressure without adding any particles.

workedExamples.title: Read the live gas state
workedExamples.intro: Use the current box as your evidence. The particles, wall-hit cues, pressure gauge, and graphs all come from the same state, so each calculation can be checked against what you see.

items.title: Calculate the current pressure
items.prompt: For the current gas state with $N = {{particleCountValue}}$, $T = {{temperatureValue}}$, and $V = {{volumeValue}}$, use the live readouts and the ideal-gas relation to find the pressure.

steps.label: 3. Check the answer against the wall hits

applyAction.label: Compare with the compressed box
applyAction.observationHint: The particles are not faster, but the smaller box makes wall hits more frequent, so the pressure gauge and pressure-volume graph both rise.

items.title: Explain the current pressure microscopically
items.prompt: For the current state, use the average speed and wall collision rate to explain why the pressure has its present value.

steps.label: 3. Link the collisions to the pressure reading

applyAction.label: Compare with a hotter box
applyAction.observationHint: The box size and particle count stay fixed, but the speed cue lengthens, the wall-hit rate rises, and the pressure-temperature graph moves upward.

miniChallenge.prompt: Two sealed boxes have the same particle number and the same volume, but Box A is hotter than Box B. Which box has the higher pressure, and what changes on the particle level explain it?
miniChallenge.prediction: Decide whether heating changes only particle speed, or both particle speed and the wall-hit pattern.
miniChallenge.answer: Box A has the higher pressure.
miniChallenge.explanation: At fixed $N$ and $V$, a hotter gas has faster particles. They hit the walls harder and also cross the box more often, so the pressure rises.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows motion streaks and the average-speed chip.
whatToNotice: Raising temperature lengthens the motion streaks before you even look at the graphs.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the temperature-pressure link visible on the stage.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows how packed the box is with density shading and a density chip.
whatToNotice: Reducing volume or adding particles increases density even if the average speed stays the same.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the $N/V$ part of the law visible.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows wall flashes and the wall collision rate.
whatToNotice: Pressure rises when wall hits become more frequent, not only when particles move faster.
overlays.whyItMatters: It connects the pressure gauge to particle-wall collisions.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the current pressure next to the live wall-hit rate.
whatToNotice: Two setups can have similar pressure even when one is hotter and the other is more crowded.
overlays.whyItMatters: It reminds you that pressure is an outcome, not the whole microscopic story.

graphs.description: Change only volume. This is the cleanest graph for seeing how compression raises pressure.
Change only temperature. Use this graph to check that hotter gas gives higher pressure at fixed $N$ and $V$.
Change only particle number. This shows that more particles raise pressure even when the average speed stays about the same.
Change only temperature. This graph shows how faster particles reach the walls more often.

noticePrompts.intro: Use one prompt at a time. Change one variable, then check the stage, the pressure gauge, and the matching graph.

items.text: Reducing only the volume raises pressure even though the speed cue stays about the same. In the smaller box, the same particles reach the walls more often.
items.tryThis: Switch between Room baseline and Compressed box. Compare the motion streaks, wall-hit flashes, and the pressure-volume graph.
items.whyItMatters: It separates the volume effect from the temperature effect.

items.text: Raising only the temperature lengthens the motion streaks and increases wall-hit rate, so pressure rises even though box size and particle count stay fixed.
items.tryThis: Hover a cool point and a warm point on the collision-temperature graph, then watch the preview values update on the stage.
items.whyItMatters: It links the pressure-temperature law to particle speed and collisions.

items.text: Adding particles at fixed temperature raises pressure without changing the average-speed cue much. More particles means more wall hits each second.
items.tryThis: Switch between Room baseline and More particles same box, then compare the density cue and the pressure-particle-count graph.
items.whyItMatters: It prevents the shortcut that every pressure change must come from temperature.

items.text: Hotter same box and More particles same box can reach similar pressures. The gauge can match while the speed cue and density cue tell different microscopic stories.
items.tryThis: In compare mode, keep one setup hotter and the other more crowded until the two pressure readings are close.
items.whyItMatters: It shows why pressure alone does not determine temperature or particle number.

items.text: Read the ideal gas law as one summary of speed, crowding, and box size, not as four unrelated symbols.
items.tryThis: Hold two variables fixed, move one slider, and use the matching response graph to explain the pressure change.
items.whyItMatters: It turns the law into a causal model instead of a formula to memorize.

predictionMode.intro: Predict before you move the slider. Each scenario asks which microscopic part of the pressure story is changing.

items.prompt: Starting from Room baseline, you reduce only the volume while keeping the same particle count and temperature. Which statement best matches the bench?
items.observationHint: The motion streaks stay similar, but the wall-hit cue and pressure gauge both increase.

items.prompt: Starting from Room baseline, you raise only the temperature. What should happen to the pressure?
items.observationHint: The motion streaks lengthen, wall hits become more frequent, and the pressure-temperature graph rises.

items.prompt: Starting from Room baseline, you increase only particle count. Which statement best matches the bench?

challengeMode.intro: Use the live gas box to hit pressure targets. The checks read the current state, so you have to create the right pressure for the right reason.

items.title: Double the pressure by compression
items.prompt: Start from Room baseline and lower only the volume until the pressure is about double while temperature and particle count stay near the baseline values.
items.successMessage: You doubled the pressure by compression alone. The particles were not individually faster; they were just reaching the walls more often in the smaller box.

setup.note: Do not use temperature or particle count to help. The point is to raise pressure by compression alone.

hints: Use the pressure-volume graph first. Halving the baseline volume is the cleanest route.
If temperature drifts away from the baseline, you are changing two causes at once.

items.title: Match the pressure with a different cause
items.successMessage: You matched the pressure with a different microscopic cause. Setup A used faster particles, while Setup B used more particles in the same box.

setup.note: Leave Setup A hot. Make Setup B cooler and more crowded.

hints: Keep the box size close to the same in both setups, then let particle count do the compensating work in Setup B.
The pressure badges should match before the speed cue and density cue do.

quickTest.intro: Answer from the stage and graphs, not from a memorized slogan. Each question checks whether you can explain pressure causally.

showMeAction.label: Show the compressed box
showMeAction.observationHint: The wall-hit cue strengthens even though the motion streak length stays about the same.

questions.prompt: At fixed particle count and volume, why does heating the gas raise pressure?

showMeAction.observationHint: Watch the motion streaks, wall-hit rate, and pressure graph rise together.

questions.prompt: Two boxes have the same temperature and the same volume, but Box B has more particles. Which statement is best supported by the model?

questions.explanation: Equal pressure does not fix temperature or particle number. Different combinations of speed, crowding, and box size can produce the same pressure.

questions.prompt: Which graph most directly shows how pressure changes with volume when particle number and temperature stay fixed?

paragraphs: The simulation shows a single gas box with moving particles. Motion streaks show the speed scale, density shading shows how crowded the gas is, wall flashes show collision frequency, and a pressure gauge shows the resulting pressure.

The graphs below each vary one control while holding the other two fixed. They let you connect what changes on the stage to a clean pressure-volume, pressure-temperature, pressure-particle-count, or collision-temperature trend.
This is a simplified 2D model rather than a full 3D molecular simulation. It is designed to make the ideal-gas patterns and the wall-collision explanation easy to see.

The pressure-volume graph isolates compression: with particle number and temperature fixed, smaller volume gives higher pressure. The pressure-temperature and pressure-particle-count graphs isolate the other two state-variable changes in the same way.
The collision-temperature graph is the kinetic-theory bridge. It does not plot pressure directly; it shows that hotter particles hit the walls more often, helping explain why pressure rises.
